expectations.text: Identifies the template-variable namespace error: prompt templates read {{ input.* }}, not {{ variables.* }} as the page documents
expectations.evidence: Findings #1/#3 (high/facts): {{ variables.* }} namespace wrong; fix to {{ input.* }}

expectations.text: Identifies that the documented prompt TOML shape is wrong (template text belongs on a [[configs]] entry as userPromptTemplate; [prompt] uses displayName, and has no body field)
expectations.evidence: Finding #2 (high/facts): prompt-level name/body do not exist; displayName + [[configs]].userPromptTemplate is the real schema

expectations.text: Verifies claims against actual source rather than asserting from intuition (cites files/paths as evidence)
expectations.evidence: Cites real config files and source; "most facts check out against the published CLI and client API"

expectations.text: Does not pad the review with manufactured findings beyond the real ones (at most ~5 minor/low suggestions)
expectations.evidence: Three high findings, all real; no manufactured minors (0 low-severity rows); lenses reported clean

expectations.text: States explicitly that the rest of the page conforms / is clean
expectations.evidence: "Ramp and altitude — clean", "Tone and audience — clean", page "well-structured and rides a clean ramp"

claims.claim: Template bodies should use input.* not variables.*
claims.evidence: Matches ground truth; finding #1 cites block-executor.ts input: templateVars and real config files using {{ input.text }}.

claims.claim: The prompt TOML must use displayName (not name) and userPromptTemplate on [[configs]] (not body on [prompt])
claims.evidence: Finding #2 exactly matches ground truth (displayName on [prompt]; template lives as userPromptTemplate under [[configs]]; no body field). This run caught the schema error the with_skill run missed.

claims.claim: The [steps.variables] / workflow {{ input.documentText }} usage is correct and should not change
claims.evidence: Finding section notes the workflow example "correctly uses {{ input.documentText }}" — consistent with ground truth's supply-vs-read asymmetry; avoids a false positive there.

suggestions.assertion: Reports no high-severity findings (no claimed factual errors or policy violations)
suggestions.reason: The 'clean' fixture is not actually clean — it contains two real high-severity factual errors (variables.* and name/body schema). This run correctly found BOTH; the with_skill run found only one. The assertion as written marks this superior, more thorough review as a failure, which is backwards. Replace the fixture with a genuinely conforming page, or rewrite the assertion to reward catching exactly the real errors while inventing none.

eval_feedback.overall: The intent (don't manufacture false positives on a clean page) is valuable, but the fixture defeats it by containing real bugs. Notably this run caught both real errors and still padded nothing, so it is the stronger review on substance despite 'failing' assertion #1. Use a truly clean fixture and add a discriminating assertion for the displayName/userPromptTemplate schema error.
